era_context: The mid-14th century: the Hundred Years' War between England and France is in its early phase; the Ming Dynasty has recently consolidated power in China (1368); the Ottoman Empire is expanding in Anatolia and the Balkans; the Black Death pandemic (1347–1353) is ravaging Europe, North Africa, and Asia; the Avignon Papacy continues in southern France; the Mamluk Sultanate controls Egypt and the Levant.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records for most polities)
Interior Americas (Aztec and Inca records are post-conquest compilations)
Central Asia and the Steppe (sparse contemporary documentation)

events.title: Battle of Crécy preparations and English military mobilisation
events.description: King Edward III of England mobilises forces and begins preparations for major military campaigns in France, part of the early Hundred Years' War. This year marks intensified English recruitment and logistical planning that would culminate in the Battle of Crécy (1346). The conflict is driven by Edward's claim to the French throne and disputes over Aquitaine. English forces begin raiding and fortifying positions in Normandy and Brittany.

sources.name: English royal records and writs (National Archives, London)

events.certainty_note: Specific military actions in 1339 are attested in royal records and near-contemporary chronicles, though Froissart's account is retrospective and sometimes conflates events across years.

events.title: Black Death continues ravaging Europe
events.description: The Black Death pandemic, which began in 1347, continues its devastating spread across Europe in 1339. Mortality rates in affected regions reach 30–60% of the population. Urban centres are particularly hard hit; entire families and communities are wiped out. The plague disrupts trade, agriculture, and social structures. Labour shortages begin to shift power dynamics between peasants and landowners. Religious responses include flagellant movements and increased persecution of Jewish communities, blamed for the plague.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus from modern epidemiological and demographic studies
Contemporary chronicles: Giovanni Boccaccio (Decameron, written 1353 but describing 1348 events); Jean de Venette (Chronicle)

events.certainty_note: The Black Death's presence and impact in 1339 is confirmed by multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources, though precise mortality figures vary by region and are often estimates.

events.title: Ottoman expansion in Anatolia and the Balkans
events.description: The Ottoman Empire, under Orhan I (r. 1323–1362), continues its territorial expansion in Anatolia and begins crossing into the Balkans. Ottoman forces capture several Byzantine fortresses and expand control over former Byzantine and Anatolian Beylik territories. This period marks the consolidation of Ottoman power and the beginning of their long conflict with the Byzantine Empire. The Ottomans establish themselves as a major regional power.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion during Orhan's reign is well-attested, but specific events in 1339 are less precisely dated. The broader pattern of expansion is confirmed; exact fortress captures and dates are sometimes conflated across years in later sources.

events.title: Avignon Papacy under Pope Benedict XII
events.description: Pope Benedict XII (r. 1334–1342) continues the Avignon Papacy, the period when the papal seat is located in Avignon rather than Rome. Benedict XII is known for his efforts at church reform and his attempts to address corruption within the clergy. He issues various bulls and decrees aimed at improving monastic discipline. The Avignon Papacy remains a source of controversy and criticism, with many viewing the papal residence in France as a sign of papal weakness and French political influence over the Church.

sources.name: Papal bulls and official documents (Vatican Archives)
Petrarch, Letters (contemporary criticism of the Avignon Papacy)

events.certainty_note: Benedict XII's papacy and his reform efforts are well-documented in papal records and contemporary correspondence.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty decline and Zhu Yuanzhang's early campaigns
events.description: In China, the Yuan Dynasty (Mongol rule) is in decline. Zhu Yuanzhang, who would found the Ming Dynasty, is beginning his military campaigns to overthrow Yuan rule. In 1339, Zhu is consolidating power in the lower Yangtze region and recruiting followers. The Yuan Dynasty faces multiple rebellions and internal instability. Zhu's rise represents the beginning of the end for Mongol rule in China, though the final conquest of Yuan territories would take decades.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Ming Veritable Records, compiled after 1368)

events.certainty_note: Zhu Yuanzhang's early campaigns are attested in Ming records, but precise dating of events in 1339 is uncertain. The Ming Shilu was compiled retrospectively and may conflate or reorder events.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of 1339 as a turning point in the Hundred Years' War is sometimes overstated in popular histories. While military preparations were underway, the major battles and decisive moments came later (Crécy in 1346, Poitiers in 1356). The year itself was not a major military engagement. Additionally, the Black Death's dating is sometimes confused: the plague began in 1347, not 1339, though its effects were building in 1339 as it spread from Asia toward Europe. Some sources conflate Ottoman expansion across multiple years; specific fortress captures attributed to 1339 in some chronicles may have occurred in adjacent years.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1339 is moderate but heavily weighted toward Western European sources (England, France, papal records). Chinese sources are available but often retrospective. Ottoman sources are later compilations. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Central Asia have virtually no contemporary written records for this year. The surviving sources reflect the biases of literate elites: military and political events are well-documented, while economic and social conditions are inferred from fragmentary evidence. The Black Death's impact is heavily documented in European sources but less so in Asian and African sources, creating a skewed picture of its global significance.

graph_edges.note: English military preparations in 1339 culminated in the Battle of Crécy in 1346
The Black Death pandemic that began in 1347 was already spreading toward Europe in 1339
Ottoman expansion in the 14th century eventually led to the fall of Constantinople in 1453
Zhu Yuanzhang's early campaigns in 1339 eventually led to the founding of the Ming Dynasty in 1368
